speaker: interviewer
text: Slate, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Today is November 5th, 1995. We are in the home survivor, Fred Efraim Hoffman. We are here in Denver, Colorado.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stand by [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, Fred, you talked about that first day at Auschwitz. And you weren't tattooed, but you remember your number you were given very clearly. What are some other things you remember from that day? What other things were going on and what were you being told?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [SIGHS] we were being told that we are going to be taken in into other places in the working camps. First, let me tell you this, they give me a number, and we had to wear that number. We didn't have no names anymore. There were no names whatsoever.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that time, they told us they would call us by the numbers and we have to report right away. So [SIGHS] when I get that number, we put it on our coat, and that was it. No names for-- anymore. We were just going by numbers.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were given a very little food to eat. They gave us a loaf of bread for eight people, a small loaf of bread. A little bit of coffee in the morning. At noon, they give us some watery soup out of spinach, grass, maybe a little potato. And at nighttime, we had again a little soup, and that was the whole food for the whole day. We had to run into the barracks to grab a blanket. The blankets were all piled up by the door. Some of the people were grabbing two, and if you came the last one, you were out of luck. Nobody to complain to. Nobody to talk to. You had to sleep without a blanket. Thank God it was gettin' kind of late in the spring, so we could push through somehow. [SIGHS] That life was going on like that for about a week and a half. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We could see and smell the air, as I said before, from the burned bodies. It smelled very bad. We see in the smog, we seeing where they burned the bodies. And then they called next day, after a week and a half, my number. They put us out in a-- outside in the, in the yard. And they started calling numbers, and we had to go and line up. We didn't know what they would do to us. They called my number. They called my father's number, he should rest in peace. And they called out about 3,000 numbers and we had to line up in groups. They took us out of there. We had to march to the train, back to the train. And they took us to Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the approximate date, Fred?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came in Buchenwald around-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --about the beginning of, of May. Hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: About the 15th of May, we arrived to Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So when you were put onto the train, who, who else was put on the train with you? Do you remember any of the other people that were with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Only-- a lot of the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was always with me, thank god. I don't know how, because we were always going together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your mother and your sisters? What, what had happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as soon we walked out of the train, that was the last time I saw them, because they were separated in that time. And we walked in the line. They went to one side, and the men went to the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea what took place with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I am sure that they gassed them and burned them. They cremated them, because my sister was only nine years old. And my mother would never give up the child. So they took the mother with my sister. And they gassed them. I found out afterwards, because they never came out of there. I never seen them since that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they called my father's number and my number, and thank god we were together. And we came into Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewee
text: Buchenwald was a big camp too. They didn't have any buildings. They had only those canvas tents, big ones. And we were assigned to certain numbers. They all had numbers on those camps, on those, on those tents. We slept there on the floor. No mattresses, no nothing. We had a little blanket to cover ourselves. They were taking us every day to one big ditch. There were a lot of rocks, big stones. And we-- we had to carry those to the camp. That was about two miles. Every day we had to take those big rocks, put on our shoulders, and walk with it to the camp, drop it there. What they did with them, I have no idea. Just to make us work. Our mind was getting weaker and weaker. We'd start to lose hope. We didn't know how long we will be there. We didn't know what is going to be with us. There was a lot of people there. Nobody knew the answer. They gave us very little food. The same thing as we had in Auschwitz. After a week and a half or two weeks of being there, that was close to end of May. They came out with a big loud speakers. And they said, we are going to take you-- in German-- to the labor camp. We need people to work. And we will go to the transport, and it will take a few days. And you will be OK there. [SIGHS] Again, we are on the train, squeezed together like sardines. We arrived to Reinsdorf. That was the name of the camp. We came to Reinsdorf. It was not a big camp, about 3 to 3-and-a-half thousand people. They assigned us to the barracks again. You'll be there, and you'll go there. And we had our barrack number. I can't remember what number that was. And they told us, from here on, you will be marching to a factory. You will be working there. You will leave early in the morning when the sun rises, and you will come back when the sun sets, around seven o'clock in the evening.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was still with my daddy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Still with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was still with my father. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you this too, because now you've gone from Auschwitz to Buchenwald to Reinsdorf. You had talked earlier about, in the ghetto, you were able to continue praying and, and continue, you know, your religious rituals. Were you able to do that in the camps as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: But--

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we gave up prayer for a while

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your belief in, in God?

speaker: interviewee
text: But in my heart it was, I'll survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: How and when, maybe yes, maybe no. When I arrived in Reinsdorf, in that camp, I still thought, it's not so bad. Someday we will be alive. Not all of us, but some of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father? What was he saying at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: The father was thinking the same way. He said he doesn't think he will make it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was only 49 years old. My mother was 44. [BACKGROUND NOISE] They assigned us at Reinsdorf to certain kommandos. They said, you will be going to this kommando, you will go on this kommando. That means groups. And when we call your kommando, you better be there right away. And we will send you out to work. I was assigned to a cable kommando. A cable kommando is electric cables, like they have on those big wheels, on those big rolls. We had to dig a ditch, but I'll come to that yet. The next day the Germans came in and they said, I need 1,000 people to work for me. They called the cable kommando. We were all out there in the groups. As soon as they said cable kommando, we already knew where we had to go. They came in front by the gate. They counted out 500 people, or whatever. The German took us, and we were walking, marching to the factory. I was working in a gasoline factory. It was called Brabag. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We-- it was about four and a half to five miles march, one way. And we were about 250, 300 people, in a line of four, marching down the street to the factory. When we came to the gate they counted how many this, this SS takes, how many he takes us out. The same amount he had to bring back in the camp. We were counted going out of the gate. We were counted coming back in the gate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the marches there, because it's quite a distance. Were they-- was it a march that would go very fast? And would they-- how would they treat along-- along that way?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were, I'll tell you. In the beginning, we could march. We don't have to walk too fast, just walk normal. On every few lines, we had the SS with the rifles, walking with us. So you could not run away, you couldn't do nothing. We walked into the factory there, and they told us what to do. Dig ditches. They gave us shovels. And then we had to take that cable from that big wheel, and put on our shoulder and stretch it. Put it down so they connect it somehow. The German engineers were connecting those electric cables. And that was our work we were assigned to. We were doing this day after day. Now, if you were tall, in the back was you-- of you, in the back was a shorter person, you carried the whole weight of that cable on your shoulder. The other one was making himself he carries, so you had twice as much pain on that shoulder. They made us walking faster and running with it. They were kicking us. They said, you have to do it fast. When we were working on those ditches, they assigned one of our guys to be a kapo. He was the-- a mediator between us and the SS. If we had anything to say, we said to the kapo. The kapo went and said it to the SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the kapo Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave him another slice of bread. That was his reward for being a kapo. And the kapo was supposed to watch us and scream at us. And they were laughing. They had fun. They said, well, one Jew screams at another. The kapo said, I have no choice. They pulled me out and they told me I have to do that. Coming back at night, marching back those four, four and a half miles, we came back tired. They counted us by the gate. They let us in. We had our little soup. And they did not let us in the barracks. We stood outside until about 10, 11 o'clock at night. After all the groups from different places, they came together, there was an appell, it's called. They had to count if all the people, while they went out during the day, they are back in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everyone had to stand there until all the groups returned?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were standing there. We couldn't sit down. And they came. The big SS counted everybody. He had the number, then they let us in in the barracks. We finally went to sleep. Midnight, appell-- appell again. They want to count again to see if everybody is there. We had to go outside again, stay outside another two hours. They did it just to, to hurt us. We had a blockalteste. That means one guy who was taking care of the barrack while we were sleeping. You were not-- you were not supposed to walk around. You were was supposed to be in your place. If you walk around, you got punished. They beat you or they sent you outside somewhere and you got beaten up. That was going on for months, and months, and months. That was our hard labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you. What would have happened if, when they did these counts, if there were people missing? What would have taken place, and did that ever happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a good question. If there were people missing, they kept us outside there until they accounted where that person is. They would not let us in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So until they could account.

speaker: interviewee
text: If they found out that he died, or he ran away, or he was hiding out, or where he was, they would not let us in. Once they had the results of him, we don't know what, then they let us in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember any instances of, of people that were resistant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There were some people, when they lost their mind, and they did some bad things like they tried to fight or they tried to run away. They didn't care if they will get killed or no. They got caught by the SS. We had a thing where they hangin' them in the middle of the, of the appell place. They used to catch those guys. Now, people start to lose their mind and they go crazy. They didn't know what was going on with them. They used to catch them. They called all the people out of the buildings. Appell again. Take that guy, pull him up there, hang him in front of us, that we could, we could all see what will happen to us if we don't follow the orders. [SIGHS] The food was very bad. A lot of people were big smokers. They used to smuggle in cigarettes from outside. You know, like my father, should rest in peace, he was a big smoker. A lot of people were like that. They used to-- somehow they used to bring in a cigarette. They used to light up one cigarette, and about five, people were standing around there, just smelling the, the smoke, getting a little taste of it, the feeling. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How, how were they able to smuggle in the cigarettes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they-- for somehow they put it in the, in the-- somehow in the clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But where would they have gotten them from?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were working in different places. They were working in the fields. They were working in the cities. And they could get if from some of the Germans, you know? They could-- they could steal it, or do something, or, or, or bring in something. And for a cigarette, you could get a slice of bread, because people were not smoking some of them. And they were selling the cigarette for a slice of bread. And the people, while they had only the slice of bread, rather give the bread up just to have the cigarette. So it was going on, a few things. Not too often though.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any women in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: None at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No women at all. All men.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the last time up, until this time, that you had seen women was when you were separated at Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were all-- Absolutely. All the way. We were not together with women until we arrived to, to Theresienstadt later on. If somebody got sick, [SIGHS] they had a hospital. They called it revier.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's say people got sick, infections. They couldn't work anymore. They sent them to that building there, the revier, that hospital. There was no medication. There was no doctors. They collected a few hundred people, and they sent them out to get gassed, and burned, and things like that. From the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the hospital. Now I'll tell you another thing. It was in the middle of the summer, in 1944. We were marching to the factory to work. I was with that cable kommando. We had to walk through a little village from the factory-- from the camp to the factory. Those Germans wanted to hurt us, from the village. They went and they crumbled bricks in tiny little pieces, and they spread it out in the village about a mile and a half, all over the street. Before we came there, those SS made us stop, make us take our shoes off, and walk barefoot over those crumbled little bricks. It was like needles. You had to go through it. Otherwise, they were beating the hell out of you. We all went through. Blood was running from our feet, for everybody. When we walked through that thing, that-- those bricks, that mile and a half, we put our shoes on, walked into the factory. And the same thing on the way back from the factory. And they were standing outside and just laughing at us, the civilians there. They had fun out of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not the SS, but-- [CLEARS THROAT]

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me. The people-- The people.

speaker: interviewer
text: --from the town. The villagers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, right. They had fun, because, you know, we were jumping around. We couldn't walk for certain. And, and, and, and they did it on their own. Nobody told them to do that. The decided they want us-- they want to hurt us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the SS were OK with that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. They agreed. Sure. They made us take the shoes off. We had to stop, take all the shoes off, and walk through, and then put the shoes back on. It was wooden shoes, what we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were working in the factory, Fred, were they feeding you during the day, during your workday?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would they give you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stopped for lunch. And they gave us a little spinach, or grass, a little potato for soup. Just, just a little soup. That's all we had. No bread, no nothing. We had in our container, our little dish, a little soup. And that's what we had. I had one SS, he kind of felt sorry for me. He is the one that was watching us. They used to eat lunch too, when we ate lunch. But they were sitting far away there somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what they were getting fed?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were getting soup and meat, and much better stuff. Bread. He-- one time he called me like this. And I go over to him. He says take this canteen and go and wash it out. There's water.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went and I opened it. And I see that there are crumbs of bread and a little soup. And I ate it. And he was watching me. And he didn't say nothing. I washed up his dish, his canteen. I gave it to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: He did this a few times a week. He kind of felt sorry for me, and he did it on purpose. He left a little food in that canteen so I can have something to eat. But he was afraid. If somebody catches him, he will be in trouble. So I had to do it very secretly. So I would just take it and eat it up fast and then wash it. There were some good ones that felt sorry for you, but they couldn't do nothing about it. They had to follow the orders. We would come in the morning, when we were getting our bread, when it's a holiday, some German holiday, they used to give us one loaf of bread for six people. So it was a little heavier slice. Or they gave us a double portion.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went one time and I ate up one slice of bread. And I took the other slice of bread, and I thought, I'll eat it the next day. I went to sleep and I put it under my pillow. Nobody saw it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thought, tomorrow I'll have a piece, a double portion. I'll be better off, I'll be-- which I could have eaten up anything in that time. But I-- I thought, I'll save it. When I got up in the morning I looked for it. It was gone. Somebody stole it. When you get the bread in the morning, the people were so wild that you had to eat it so fast, otherwise somebody would just come and grab it out of your hand. And-- and they would, and they would eating it up. You didn't even-- you couldn't even keep it for eatin' it slowly. You had to eat it very fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fred, do you remember during this time, your health, and, and, and how you were feeling, and your father's as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm coming to it now.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK,

speaker: interviewee
text: I was feelin'-- feelin' weak. I was feeling plenty weak. I was hungry. When we came back from the camp, from the-- yeah-- from the factory, they used to take in 20, 30 people into the kitchen to peel potatoes, carrots, to prepare for next day. We would run into the kitchen after the appell, after they counted us. Try to be the first one so they will take you in. If you were lucky, they took in 30 people. The rest had to go back out. Once in a while, I succeeded. So I went in there and I was peeling, and I was-- could eat some of those, of those potatoes. And the potato peels, they were thrown out in the back of the kitchen there. We used to get up at night time, sneak out from the building around to that big pile of peels from the potatoes, grab in that little canteen, run back to the barrack and eat it. Unwashed, uncooked, just as it is. My father starts feeling pretty weak. And-- [CRYING] [DRINKS] [CRYING] He was terribly-- [CRYING] He doesn't have no more power. He's gotten sick. He cannot walk. I look at him. His face was swollen. He legs were swollen. He couldn't walk anymore. Also, when we were walking to the factory to work, to that gasoline factory, if somebody couldn't walk, you tried to help them, they wouldn't let us do that. He was getting in the back-- the back of the line, they just shot him there and left him there. My father started getting weaker and weaker. [CRYING] I could see he wasn't going to make it. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I told him to go up to that hospital. Maybe he will be there a couple days, he will feel better, he will come out. So he went. He says to the SS, he says, I'm sick. I can't walk. He said go to the revier, go to that hospital. So he was there a couple days. And a few days I came from the factory, and after night time, when and I came back from the work, I went to visit him, to see how he's doing. That was going on for a few days.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] One day he asked to me, I have a piece of bread. Go sell it for a cigarette. I said, I don't know what to sell it for. I said I don't know who to go to. He says, go sell it for a cigarette. He didn't eat the bread the whole day. He was waiting for me to come home, get a cigarette. Get a piece of bread, and sell it for a cigarette.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was walking around. I see a guy there, approximate. I went over. I said do you have a cigarette? I'll give you a slice of bread. He said yeah, here it is. So I went and I gave him the cigarette for my father. He said the cigarette will do me better than the bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it wasn't getting any better. After a week or so, I come home and I run in to visit him and the whole building was empty. All the sick people were gone. They took all the sick people away again, and they got rid of them. They burned them. They gassed them. They killed them out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here I was left all alone A soul with these strange people in a strange world.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] I didn't know anybody. And I was alone left, and just pushed through the days and see what will happen. And that was going on for quite a few other months. We were there about, I would say, about 10 months in that camp. And I kept on going to that factory, and we kept on working. And, and for some reason, it was OK.
